---
title: "Google Colab"
description: "Run Python in a browser and learn the notebook workflow without installing software."
execute:
  echo: true
categories: [getting-started, beginner]
order: 60
---
::: {.lesson-meta}
**Level:** Beginner
**Estimated time:** 20–30 minutes
**You will learn:** Run Python in a browser and learn the notebook workflow without installing software.
**Practice in:** Google Colab and browser notebooks
:::

## The problem this lesson solves

You can learn Python before installing anything on your computer. Google Colab
runs notebooks in the browser, which makes it a friendly place to practice code,
write notes, see output, and share work.

Colab is especially useful at the beginning because it removes setup problems.
You can focus on code first, then later learn local tools such as VS Code and
Jupyter.

## What a notebook is

A notebook is a document made of **cells**. A cell can contain code or notes.
When you run a code cell, Python executes that cell and shows the output below
it.

## How notebook cells run

::: {.concept-diagram}
A notebook keeps code, output, and explanation close together.

```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
%%| eval: true
flowchart TD
  notebook["Notebook"] --> markdown["Text/Markdown cell<br/>explain the idea"]
  notebook --> code["Code cell<br/>name = 'Ada'"]
  code --> runtime["Runtime<br/>Python process"]
  runtime --> output["Output under the cell<br/>Welcome, Ada!"]
```

The runtime remembers values from cells that have already run. If results become
confusing, restart the runtime and run cells from top to bottom.
:::

## Vocabulary in plain language

| Word | Beginner-friendly meaning |
| --- | --- |
| Notebook | A document containing notes, code cells, and output. |
| Cell | One editable block in a notebook. |
| Code cell | A cell that Python can run. |
| Markdown cell | A cell for formatted notes, headings, and explanations. |
| Runtime | The Python session connected to the notebook. |
| Output | What appears below a code cell after it runs. |

## First notebook exercise

Open <https://colab.new>. Create or use the first code cell and run:

```python
name = "Ada"
print(f"Welcome, {name}!")
```

## Step-by-step explanation

1. `name = "Ada"`
   - Python creates the string `"Ada"`.
   - The name `name` now refers to that string.
2. `print(f"Welcome, {name}!")`
   - The `f` creates an f-string.
   - `{name}` is replaced with the value `"Ada"`.
   - The output is `Welcome, Ada!`.
3. The output appears below the code cell.

## Cell order matters

A notebook can be run out of order. That is powerful, but it can also confuse
beginners.

Try this in two separate code cells.

Cell 1:

```python
name = "Ada"
```

Cell 2:

```python
print(name)
```

If you run Cell 2 before Cell 1, Python raises `NameError` because `name` does
not exist yet. Run Cell 1 first, then Cell 2.

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
If notebook output stops making sense, restart the runtime and run all cells from
the top. This clears old hidden state.
:::

## Notes and code together

Add a Markdown cell above your code and write:

```markdown
## Greeting experiment

Prediction: the code will print a welcome message for Ada.
```

Good notebooks explain the purpose of the code, not just the final answer. Use
notes for predictions, observations, and questions.

## Check your understanding

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "colab-basics-checkpoint",
  "title": "Colab basics checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "notebook-meaning",
      "prompt": "What is a notebook?",
      "options": [
        "A document with cells for notes, code, and output.",
        "Only a Python package installer.",
        "A folder that cannot run code.",
        "A terminal command only."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Notebooks combine explanation, executable code, and results."
    },
    {
      "id": "runtime-meaning",
      "prompt": "What is the runtime in a Colab notebook?",
      "options": [
        "The Python session connected to the notebook.",
        "A Markdown heading.",
        "The name of every variable.",
        "A saved image only."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The runtime runs code and remembers values created by cells."
    },
    {
      "id": "output-location",
      "prompt": "Where does output from a code cell usually appear?",
      "options": [
        "Below the cell after it runs.",
        "Only in a separate email.",
        "Inside the browser address bar.",
        "Nowhere, because notebooks hide all output."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Notebook output appears near the code that produced it."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Practice routine in Colab

Use this three-cell pattern for course exercises:

1. **Prediction cell**: write what you expect in a Markdown cell.
2. **Original code cell**: run the lesson example unchanged.
3. **Experiment cell**: copy the code and change one thing.

Example experiment:

```python
name = "Grace"
print(f"Welcome, {name}!")
```

## Common Colab problems and fixes

| Problem | What to try first |
| --- | --- |
| `NameError` for a value defined earlier | Run the earlier cell, or restart and run from top. |
| Output looks old | Run the current cell again. |
| Too much output | Stop or interrupt the running cell. |
| Notebook is not saved | Save a copy in your own Drive or download a copy. |

## Check your understanding again

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "colab-workflow-checkpoint",
  "title": "Colab workflow checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "cell-order",
      "prompt": "Why can running cells out of order cause NameError?",
      "options": [
        "A later cell may use a name before the earlier cell creates it.",
        "Colab cannot run strings.",
        "Markdown cells delete variables.",
        "Python refuses all notebooks with two cells."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Names exist only after the cell that creates them has run in the current runtime."
    },
    {
      "id": "restart-run-all",
      "prompt": "What should you try when notebook state becomes confusing?",
      "options": [
        "Restart the runtime and run cells from the top.",
        "Delete every code example.",
        "Change many cells at once.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Restarting clears old state and running top-to-bottom rebuilds it predictably."
    },
    {
      "id": "markdown-use",
      "prompt": "What is a good use for Markdown cells?",
      "options": [
        "Predictions, explanations, headings, and observations.",
        "Only storing secret passwords.",
        "Replacing all Python code.",
        "Installing the interpreter."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Markdown cells help learners explain what code is doing."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Key points

::: {.callout-tip title="Key points"}
- Colab lets you run Python in a browser.
- A notebook contains code cells, note cells, and output.
- The runtime remembers values from cells that have run.
- Running cells out of order can cause confusing results.
- Restarting and running from top is a reliable notebook debugging habit.
:::

## References

- Google Colab: https://colab.new
- Colab overview: https://colab.research.google.com/
- Markdown guide: https://www.markdownguide.org/basic-syntax/
